one_line_takeaway: A unified taxonomy + fair benchmark of 17 jailbreak attacks over 9 aligned LLMs and 8 defenses, showing high-ASR seed-based attacks (like LAA) are easily defended to ~0% while diverse seedless feedback attacks (PAIR, TAP) survive all eight defenses.
key_finding: No tested LLM is initially fully resistant (even well-aligned Llama3 has baseline ASR 0.39), and every model exceeds 0.55 ASR under at least one attack. LAA is the strongest attack overall (0.87 average ASR, 1.00 on DeepSeek-V3 and Vicuna, 0.55 on the hardest model Llama3.1), outperforming even white-box attacks. DeepSeek-V3 is the most vulnerable model (0.75 average ASR). The central pattern: seed-dependent attacks (human-based, heuristic-based, fine-tuning-based) achieve high ASRs but produce low-diversity prompts that defenses crush -- Prompt-Guard alone drives LAA and most seed-based methods to ~0% ASR. Conversely, methods that craft diverse, natural prompts without initial seeds (feedback-based: PAIR, TAP, and also DrAttack, COLD) stay robust; PAIR and TAP retain ASR above 0.15 even with all eight defenses deployed together. The authors conclude the community should deprioritize incremental seed-based attacks and focus on/against seedless attacks.
core_contribution: The paper (titled 'JailbreakRadar' in the camera-ready) is a systematization-and-benchmark study, not a new attack. Its own contributions are: (1) a novel six-category taxonomy of jailbreak attacks against aligned LLMs, organized by whether the attack alters the original forbidden question and how the modified prompt is produced -- human-based, obfuscation-based, heuristic-based, feedback-based, fine-tuning-based, and generation-parameter-based; (2) a unified usage policy of 16 violation categories synthesized from five major providers (Google, OpenAI, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft) and a reusable dataset of 160 forbidden questions (10 per category, Child Endangerment deliberately excluded); (3) a large-scale, controlled measurement running 17 pre-existing attacks against 9 aligned LLMs, plus ablations (time, token length, transferability, longitudinal drift) and an evaluation under 8 external defenses. The 17 attacks themselves (AIM, GCG, PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, LAA, etc.) are prior work being benchmarked -- the contribution is the unified taxonomy, dataset, and the first apples-to-apples comparison across automatic and non-automatic methods under consistently aligned settings.
threat_model: Adversary sends adversarial ('jailbreak') inputs to a safety-aligned LLM to elicit content that violates the provider's usage policy. The paper emphasizes the realistic black-box setting (no weights/gradients) but also benchmarks white-box attacks (GCG, COLD, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter) and a generation-parameter attack (GE) that only manipulates decoding parameters without any jailbreak prompt. Attacks are grouped by capability requirements (black-box access, whether they modify the original question, whether they need human-crafted initial seeds). Defender deploys external guardrails (perplexity filters, moderation classifiers, LLM-based guards, prompt rewriting/erasing).
limitations: Scope: only 17 of 200+ known attacks are evaluated, so coverage is admittedly partial. Static artifacts: the unified policy and 160-question dataset are frozen to 2024 provider policies and will age as policies evolve; only inter-category (not intra-category) differences are analyzed. Evaluation: ASR is judged automatically by GPT-4-Turbo (better than alternatives but imperfect); the authors cannot assess response *quality* or whether harmful content exceeds what is already publicly available, only that jailbreaks make elicitation easier. Potential biases: LLMs were used both to build the forbidden-question set and inside several attacks/judging, risking circularity and contamination; data leakage into newer models is possible. DeepSeek-V3 is open-source but was run in a closed-source (API) setting due to compute limits.

datasets_used: Custom forbidden-question dataset: 160 questions, 16 violation categories (10 each), Child Endangerment excluded; built from prior datasets plus LLM-generated and manually screened
Unified usage policy synthesized from 5 providers: Google, OpenAI, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft
Source datasets drawn from: AdvBench (Zou et al.) and in-the-wild jailbreak prompts (Shen et al.)
quantitative_results: 17 jailbreak attacks, 9 aligned LLMs (5 open: ChatGLM3, Llama2, Llama3, Llama3.1, Vicuna; 4 closed: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, DeepSeek-V3, PaLM2), 8 defenses
LAA: best overall attack, 0.87 average ASR; 1.00 ASR on DeepSeek-V3, Vicuna, and GPT-3.5; 0.55 on Llama3.1
DeepSeek-V3: most vulnerable model, 0.75 average ASR across all attacks
Baseline (direct forbidden question, no jailbreak): 0.42 average ASR; Llama3 baseline 0.39; Llama3.1 most robust (0.26 average across attacks)
DrAttack most broadly applicable obfuscation method: 0.63 average ASR; GE (generation-parameter) 0.68 average ASR, third overall
Per-taxonomy averages: AutoDAN 0.70, TAP 0.65, PAIR 0.64, DrAttack 0.63, AIM 0.62, GPTFuzz 0.58, GCG 0.57, MasterKey 0.56

Defense: Prompt-Guard reduces LAA and AutoDAN ASR to 0.00 (and near-0 for all seed-based methods); with all 8 defenses combined, PAIR 0.16, TAP 0.19, COLD 0.17, DrAttack 0.36 remain effective while seed-based attacks fall to ~0
Perplexity defense effective on high-perplexity prompts: cuts GCG 0.57->0.09 and Zulu 0.30->0.04; Moderation nearly ineffective across the board
baselines_compared: Direct-attack baseline: querying target LLMs with raw forbidden questions and no jailbreak (average ASR 0.42)

The 17 attacks compared against each other across the six taxonomy categories: AIM, Devmoderanti, Devmode v2 (human); Base64, Combination, Zulu, DrAttack (obfuscation); AutoDAN, GPTFuzz, LAA (heuristic); GCG, COLD, PAIR, TAP (feedback); MasterKey, AdvPrompter (fine-tuning); Generation Exploitation/GE (generation-parameter)
Judge-method baselines: authors' 3-aspect GPT-4-Turbo judge compared against string-match and prior LLM-as-a-judge, validated by human annotation
8 defenses compared: Self-Reminder, Moderation, Perplexity, Erase, Llama-Guard, Llama-Guard-2, Llama-Guard-3, Prompt-Guard (individually and all combined)
